mnemonics.title: TYPES of Learning Resources
mnemonics.content: J-P-O-M: Journals, Printed/Electronic books, Online resources, MOOCs/Courses - The 'J-P-O-M' helps recall four main resource categories.

mnemonics.title: Green IT Standard Recall
mnemonics.content: 1-4-0-1: ISO 14001 (Environment), ISO 50001 (Energy), ISO 27001 (Security) - '1-4-0-1' sequence for environmental standards.

mnemonics.title: Resource Selection Criteria
mnemonics.content: C-R-A-P: Current, Relevant, Academic, Practical - CRAP test for evaluating learning resources.

mnemonics.title: Online Learning Benefits
mnemonics.content: A-N-Y-W-H-E-R-E: Accessible, Niche content, Your pace, Hands-on, Expert instructors, E-certifications, Real-time - ANYWHERE summarizes online learning advantages.

acronyms.usage: Online courses available to unlimited participants, widely used for Green IT certification programs
Freely accessible teaching materials for Green IT sustainability education
Platform for delivering online Green IT courses and tracking progress
Professional body providing Green Computing SIG and resources

acronyms.expansion: Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers
acronyms.usage: Technical organization with Green Computing technical committee and publications
Body that publishes environmental and energy management standards like ISO 14001, 50001

visualTips.visualization: Pyramid with foundational resources (textbooks) at base, applied resources (case studies) in middle, and cutting-edge resources (journals/conferences) at top - showing progression from basics to advanced research.

Four quadrants: Visual (diagrams/videos), Auditory (podcasts/webinars), Reading/Writing (journals/textbooks), Kinesthetic (simulation labs) - each representing different learning approaches for Green IT.
Graph showing textbooks high in depth but low in currency (publication lag), journals high in both, online articles high in currency but variable depth, conference proceedings moderate in both.

analogies.analogy: Just as a balanced diet needs proteins (core textbooks), vegetables (journals), supplements (online resources), and variety (case studies), Green IT education needs diverse resources for complete knowledge.
Journals are like 24-hour news for Green IT research - constantly updating with new findings, peer-reviewed for accuracy, essential for staying current.
Textbooks are like the foundation of a building - they provide the stable, structured base upon which advanced knowledge is constructed.
Case studies are like cooking recipes - they show step-by-step how real organizations prepared successful sustainable IT solutions, not just theory.
